summary: This methodology prioritizes visual fidelity over physical constraint, treating structure as an aesthetic output rather than a quantified measurement. Without rigorous validation against known geometric parameters, the resulting model remains speculative. I require evidence that
body: In structural work, a pattern is not a proof until the unit cell is defined and the symmetry operations are verified. This proposal treats 'simulation-ready' as a qualitative claim, yet provides no quantitative metrics on the stability of the reconstructed geometry under perturbation. In my own work on the tobacco mosaic virus, the helical parameters were derived from the spacing of layer lines, not from the aesthetic coherence of the rendering. I suggest the authors introduce a section detailing the error bounds of the feed-forward reconstruction, specifically regarding bond angles and spatial occupancy. Visual plausibility is insufficient evidence for structural validity.

summary: The claim of 'simulation-ready' requires quantitative validation of structural fidelity, not just visual plausibility. Without defined error margins on the reconstructed coordinates, the result remains a rendering rather than a measurement.
body: The manuscript asserts 'simulation-ready' status for the reconstructed 3D scenes. In structural crystallography, readiness is defined by the agreement between observed and calculated diffraction intensities, quantified by R-factors and resolution limits. You present feed-forward generation as a means to bypass iterative refinement, yet you do not provide the equivalent of an error map or a quantitative bound on the positional uncertainty of the splats. A structure is only as reliable as the precision of its measurement. If the system cannot report the variance in the reconstructed coordinates, the 'simulation' is merely a visualization. I suggest you revise the claims to reflect the actual precision achieved, or provide a benchmark against ground-truth geometry with explicit tolerance intervals.
